title: Chromosome behaviour in mitosis
paperName: Paper 1 Multiple Choice & Paper 2 AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Cambridge 9700 Paper 2 — Chromosome behaviour in mitosis (5.2). A-Level Notes diagram-backed lesson with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Describe the key events and appearance of chromosomes in each stage of mitosis (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase).
Explain the importance of mitosis in growth, repair, and asexual reproduction, ensuring genetic identity.
Outline the role of spindle fibres in chromosome separation and understand the terms haploid (n) and diploid (2n).

simpleExplanation.title: Mitosis Made Simple
simpleExplanation.summary: Mitosis is how one 'parent' cell divides into two genetically identical 'daughter' cells. It’s essential for growth, repairing damaged tissues, and replacing old cells, ensuring all new cells have the exact same set of chromosomes.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of mitosis like making a perfectly identical photocopy of a crucial document. You start with one complete document (the parent cell), duplicate its contents precisely, and end up with two exact copies (daughter cells), each with the full information.
steps: Visualise each stage: Draw diagrams for Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase.
Learn key events: For each stage, identify 2-3 defining actions (e.g., chromosomes condense, line up, separate).
Understand the 'why': Connect mitosis to its functions – growth, repair, asexual reproduction.
Practise labelling: Use past paper diagrams to identify stages and label structures like spindle fibres and centromeres.

sections.content: Welcome to this premium revision lesson on **chromosome behaviour in mitosis**! This fundamental process of cell division is crucial for growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction across all eukaryotes. Mastering the intricate dance of **chromosomes** through **prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase** is key to excelling in your Cambridge 9700 Biology exams. Key points align with **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** 9700 Biology chapter **5** (topic **5.2**). Notes and diagrams from **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** chapter **5**, topic **5.2** (4 pages).
A-Level Notes — topic content
1) G 1 phase

• cells make RNA, enzymes and other proteins needed for growth.
• at the end of G 1 phase, the cell becomes committed to dividing or not.
2) S phase

• synthesis of DNA.
• the DNA in the nucleus replicates so that each chromosome consists of 2 identical chromatids: DNA replication.
3) G 2 phase

• the cell continues to grow.
• new DNA is checked, and any errors are repaired.
• preparations are made to start cell division e.g., a sharp increase in production of the protein tubulin is observed which is needed to make microtubules for mitotic spindle M phase (mitosis) Nuclear division that produces 2 genetically identical daughter cells.
• Maintains genetic stability.
• Ensures cells retain function.
• Maintains chromosome number Functions of mitosis.
• cell replacement.
• repair of tissues.
1) Prophase

• centrosomes migrate to opposite poles of mitotic spindle.
• chromatin starts to condense.
• nuclear envelope and nucleolus begin to disappear.
• centrosomes and migrate to opposite poles of the cell and form poles of mitotic spindle.
• asters and spindle fibres form.
2) Metaphase

• centrosomes are at poles of the cell and organise the production of spindle microtubules.
• chromosomes line up across equator of the spindle.
• each chromosome splits at centromere.
• chromosomes start to be pulled apart by microtubules.
3) Anaphase

• chromatids move to opposite poles, centromeres are first pulled by microtubules.
• spindle fibres contract and chromosomes are pulled to the opposite ends.
4) Telophase

• nuclear envelope and nucleolus reforming.
• Cytokinesis in plant cells.
• cell plate forms across equator of cell; cellulose, cell wall laid down.
• cytoplasm is hence divided into 2 Image: https://courses.lumenlearning.com/ Cytokinesis in animal cells.
• contractile ring forms, pushing the equator of cell inwards, forming a cleavage furrow.
• this cleavage furrow deepens as active filaments in the ring contract.
• eventually the cell splits into 2 Image: https://courses.lumenlearning.com/ Differences in plant and animal cell mitosis.
• plant cells don’t have any visible centriole or aster.
1) Centromere

• needed for separation of chromatids during mitosis.
• site of attachment of spindle microtubules.
• each metaphase chromosome has 2 kinetochores at its centromere and microtubules - kinetochore – protein molecules which bind to DNA in centromere and microtubules - bundles of microtubules called spindle fibres extend from kinetochores to poles of spindle during mitosis.
2) Centrosome

• an organelle found in animal cells that acts as the microtubule organising centre (MTOC) for construction of the spindle.
• present at the poles of cell Image: https://www.qsstudy.com/biology/centrosome-definition-function.
The Stages of Mitosis: A Continuous Cycle

While mitosis is a continuous process, biologists divide it into four distinct phases for easier study: **prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase**. Each stage is characterised by specific changes in the nucleus and cytoplasm, particularly the organisation and movement of chromosomes. Prior to mitosis, during the interphase S phase, DNA replication occurs, resulting in each chromosome consisting of two identical **sister chromatids** joined at the **centromere**.
Prophase

This is often the longest phase of mitosis.
*   **Chromatin condensation**: The long, diffuse chromatin fibres begin to coil and condense, becoming visible as distinct, thread-like chromosomes under a light microscope. Each chromosome is clearly seen to consist of two sister chromatids.
*   **Nuclear envelope breakdown**: The nuclear envelope disassembles into small vesicles.
*   **Centrosome movement and spindle formation**: In animal cells, the two **centrosomes** (each containing a pair of **centrioles**) move to opposite poles of the cell. From these centrosomes, **spindle fibres** (microtubules) begin to extend, forming the **mitotic spindle**. Plant cells lack centrioles but still form a spindle.
Metaphase

This is a relatively short phase.
*   **Chromosome alignment**: The condensed chromosomes migrate to the cell’s equatorial plate, known as the **metaphase plate** (or equatorial plate), which is equidistant from the two spindle poles.
*   **Spindle fibre attachment**: Each sister chromatid's centromere is now attached to spindle fibres originating from opposite poles. These fibres, specifically **kinetochore microtubules**, pull on the centromeres, ensuring the chromosomes are correctly aligned and under tension.
Anaphase

This is the shortest but most dramatic phase.
*   **Sister chromatid separation**: The centromeres holding sister chromatids together divide. Each chromatid is now considered an individual chromosome.
*   **Chromosome migration**: The newly separated chromosomes are pulled by the shortening spindle fibres towards opposite poles of the cell. This movement is rapid and ensures an identical set of chromosomes moves to each pole.
Telophase

Essentially the reverse of prophase.
*   **Chromosome decondensation**: Once at the poles, the chromosomes begin to uncoil and decondense, returning to their diffuse chromatin state.
*   **Nuclear envelope reformation**: New nuclear envelopes form around the two sets of chromosomes at each pole, creating two distinct nuclei.
*   **Spindle disassembly**: The spindle fibres depolymerise.
*   **Cytokinesis initiation**: The division of the cytoplasm, **cytokinesis**, typically begins during late anaphase or telophase, ultimately pinching the parent cell into two separate daughter cells. In animal cells, a **cleavage furrow** forms; in plant cells, a **cell plate** forms.
The Significance of Mitosis

Mitosis is fundamental to life for several critical reasons:
*   **Growth**: It increases the number of cells in a multicellular organism, leading to growth in size.
*   **Repair and Replacement**: It replaces damaged or old cells (e.g., skin cells, red blood cells) and repairs tissues after injury, maintaining tissue integrity.
*   **Asexual Reproduction**: In many unicellular and some multicellular organisms, mitosis is the basis of asexual reproduction, producing offspring that are genetically identical to the parent.
*   **Genetic Identity**: Crucially, mitosis produces two **genetically identical daughter cells** from a single parent cell. This means the **diploid (2n)** chromosome number and the exact genetic make-up are maintained across generations of cells, ensuring consistent function and characteristics within an organism. **Haploid (n)** refers to cells with half the diploid chromosome number, characteristic of gametes, and is relevant to meiosis, not mitosis.

items: Chromatid – one half of two identical copies of a replicated chromosomes.
Centromere – the region of a chromosome to which the microtubules of the spindle attach, via the kinetochore, during cell division.
Telomeres – protective sequence of nucleotides found at the ends of chromosomes DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) DNA is the molecule of inheritance and is made up of a series of genes.
a gene is a section of DNA that codes for one protein.
DNA molecules are in sister chromatids so that their genes are identical.
when cells divide, one chromatid goes into one daughter cell and the other to the other daughter cell, making the daughter cells genetically identical.
size of DNA molecule – 2nm Image: https://mrleehamber119.wordpress.com/.
the total length of DNA of 46 chromosomes of an adult human is about 1.8m, this needs to be fit into a nucleus of 6μm.
therefore, the DNA is wound around the outside of proteins called histones - histone proteins – basic proteins which associate with DNA to form nucleosomes.

these histone proteins (and therefore, nucleosomes) condense DNA to chromatin - chromatin – a complex of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) and other proteins whose primary function is to compress DNA into a compact unit which will fit inside the nucleus Nucleosomes.
cap the ends of chromosomes and are needed for successful cell division.
prevent the loss of genes Image: https://www.tasciences.com/what-is-a-telomere.html.
telomeres are made of DNA with short base sequences that are repeated multiple times.

sections.question: A cell from a plant's root tip is observed undergoing mitosis. Describe the key events that occur during **anaphase** in this cell and explain the importance of precise chromosome segregation during this stage.
sections.solution:
1.  **Centromere division**: The centromeres, which hold the sister chromatids together, divide.
2.  **Sister chromatid separation**: This separation transforms each chromatid into an individual, un-replicated chromosome.
3.  **Chromosome migration**: Shortening **spindle fibres** (specifically kinetochore microtubules) pull these newly formed chromosomes towards opposite poles of the cell. The poles are defined by the organizing centres from which the spindle fibres originate.
4.  **Significance**: Precise chromosome segregation in anaphase is critical because it ensures that each new daughter cell receives an identical and complete set of genetic information. Failure to do so would result in daughter cells with an incorrect number of chromosomes (aneuploidy), which can lead to cell dysfunction, developmental abnormalities, or even cell death. This genetic identicality is essential for proper growth, tissue repair, and maintaining the organism's genetic stability.

sections.content: When drawing mitotic stages for plant cells, remember to depict a rigid cell wall and the formation of a cell plate during cytokinesis, rather than a cleavage furrow seen in animal cells. Always label your diagrams clearly, indicating the cell wall, nuclear envelope (or absence), spindle fibres, and chromosomes/chromatids, showing their correct number and morphology!

faq.q: What is chromosome behaviour in mitosis 9700?
faq.a: In Cambridge 9700 Biology, chromosome behaviour in mitosis refers to the precise, orchestrated movements and structural changes of chromosomes through prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase. This ensures that each new daughter cell receives an exact, identical set of chromosomes. You can master these stages with our free premium lessons and practice using past papers!

faq.q: Why is mitosis important for living organisms?
faq.a: Mitosis is essential for several vital processes: it enables growth in multicellular organisms by increasing cell numbers, facilitates the repair of damaged tissues and replacement of worn-out cells, and serves as the basis for asexual reproduction in many species. Its primary role is to produce genetically identical daughter cells, maintaining genetic stability.

faq.q: How do plant and animal cells differ during mitosis?
faq.a: While the fundamental chromosome movements are similar, key differences exist. Animal cells form a cleavage furrow during cytokinesis to divide the cytoplasm, and they possess centrioles within their centrosomes which organise the spindle fibres. Plant cells, however, form a cell plate to build a new cell wall between daughter cells and typically lack centrioles, forming their spindle directly from microtubule-organising centres.
